reasoning: The government of Mali, currently led by a military junta under Gen. Assimi Goïta, issued a presidential decree on May 13, 2025, completely dissolving all political parties. This action was not an isolated decree but followed the formal repeal of the long-standing Political Parties Charter by the Malian legislature just days prior on May 12, 2025. This coordinated move demonstrates a strong structural intent to dismantle political opposition. Furthermore, the junta's recent "national conference" recommended that Gen. Goïta remain in power for a five-year renewable term starting in 2025, further illustrating a transition towards long-term authoritarian consolidation, mirroring the actions of allied military juntas in Niger and Burkina Faso. Given that the decree was enacted in mid-May and the resolution date is July 1, 2025 (only about 6 weeks later), there is practically no time or political will for the junta to suddenly reverse this landmark structural decision. While there is domestic pushback and international condemnation, the junta has historically ignored these pressures (e.g., pulling out of ECOWAS). A partial reversal (e.g., passing a new restrictive charter allowing new parties to form) within 6 weeks of formally abolishing the system is highly improbable. Therefore, the probability is assessed as 0.02.
